In all dangers, whether public or personal, our first business should be to seek help from God. This is why days for national fasting and prayer are valuable. From beginning to end of seeking the Lord, we must approach him with humility for our sins, trusting only in his mercy and power. Jehoshaphat acknowledges the sovereign dominion of Divine Providence. Lord, use it on our behalf. Whom should we seek help from, whom should we trust for relief, but the God we have chosen and served? Those who use what they have for God may comfortably hope he will secure it to them. Every true believer is a son of Abraham, a friend of God; with such people the everlasting covenant is established, and every promise belongs to them. We are assured of God's love by his dwelling in human nature in the person of the Savior. Jehoshaphat mentions the temple as a token of God’s favorable presence. He pleads the injustice of his enemies. We may well appeal to God against those who render us evil for good. Though he had a great army, he said, “We have no strength without you; we rely upon you.”

The Spirit of prophecy came upon a Levite in the midst of the congregation. The Spirit, like the wind, blows where and on whom He wills. He encouraged them to trust in God. Let the Christian soldier go out against his spiritual enemies, and the God of peace will make him more than a conqueror. Our trials will prove our gain. The advantage will be all ours, but all the glory must be given to God.

Jehoshaphat exhorted his troops to firm faith in God. Faith inspires a man with true courage; nor will anything help more to establish the heart in shaking times than a firm belief of the power, and mercy, and promise of God. In all our trust in the Lord, and our praises of him, let us especially look at his everlasting mercy to sinners through Jesus Christ. Never was an army so destroyed as that of the enemy. Thus God often makes wicked people destroy one another. And never was a victory celebrated with more solemn thanksgivings.

Jehoshaphat kept close to the worship of God and did what he could to keep his people close to it. But after God had done such great things for him, given him not only victory but wealth; after this, to go and join himself with a wicked king was very ungrateful. What could he expect but that God would be angry with him? Yet it seems he took the warning; for when Ahaziah afterward pressed him to join him, he would not. Thus the alliance was broken, and the Divine rebuke had its effect, at least for a season. Let us be thankful for any losses which may have prevented the loss of our immortal souls. Let us praise the Lord who sought after us and left us not to perish in our sins.
